_meta.authoringNotes: Epidemiology from Weatherall DJ Lancet 2010 (PMID 20692526) and Modell & Darlison Bull WHO 2008. Clinical classification and management per Vichinsky et al. NEJM 2005 (PMID 16148284) and ACOG Practice Bulletin 2007. Molecular genetics reviewed in Harteveld & Higgs Orphanet J Rare Dis 2010 (PMID 20482823).

h1Title: Alpha Thalassemia: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Alpha thalassemia is an inherited hemoglobin disorder caused by deletion or mutation of one or more of the four alpha-globin genes on chromosome 16, reducing or eliminating alpha-globin chain synthesis. It is the world's most common single-gene disorder, with an estimated 300 million carriers globally, particularly prevalent in sub-Saharan Africa, the Mediterranean, Middle East, and Southeast Asia. Clinical severity ranges from the silent carrier state (one gene deleted, no symptoms) through the minor trait (two genes deleted, mild microcytic anemia) to hemoglobin H disease (three genes deleted, moderately severe hemolytic anemia) to the lethal hemoglobin Bart hydrops fetalis syndrome (four genes deleted, incompatible with life without intervention). Management depends on severity; most carriers need no treatment while HbH disease requires regular monitoring and transfusion support during crises.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 300 million carriers worldwide; gene deletion frequencies of 20–30% in parts of Southeast Asia and sub-Saharan Africa (Weatherall 2010)
keyStats.demographics: Highest prevalence in Southeast Asia, southern China, sub-Saharan Africa, Mediterranean basin, and the Middle East; increasingly seen in multiethnic Western populations through immigration
keyStats.avgAge: Symptoms in HbH disease typically apparent from infancy or early childhood; silent carriers and trait carriers identified at any age, often only on routine CBC
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 100,000+ annual births affected by major alpha thalassemia worldwide; Hb Bart hydrops fetalis causes approximately 5,000–10,000 stillbirths per year in Southeast Asia alone

definition: Alpha thalassemia (ICD-10: D56.0) is a quantitative hemoglobin disorder resulting from reduced or absent synthesis of alpha-globin chains. In humans, alpha-globin is encoded by two genes per chromosome — HBA1 and HBA2 — giving each person four functional alpha-globin genes in total. Loss of one or two genes produces mild or asymptomatic disease; loss of three genes causes hemoglobin H disease, in which excess beta-globin chains form soluble tetramers (HbH, β₄) that are unstable, produce reactive oxygen species, and cause chronic hemolytic anemia of moderate severity. Loss of all four genes leads to hemoglobin Bart hydrops fetalis (γ₄ tetramers predominate, with virtually no functional hemoglobin), a condition uniformly fatal in utero or shortly after birth without intrauterine transfusion. Alpha thalassemia is classified within the broader group of thalassemia syndromes managed by hematologists, and prenatal diagnosis is offered in all countries with a high disease burden. It sits in the differential diagnosis of any microcytic hypochromic anemia, distinguished from iron deficiency by normal or elevated ferritin and a characteristic hemoglobin analysis finding reduced or absent HbA₂ (in contrast to beta thalassemia, where HbA₂ is elevated).

typesClassification.description: One of four alpha-globin genes is non-functional. Red cell indices are essentially normal; MCV may be very mildly reduced. No clinical symptoms. Identified only through gene testing or family studies. Carrier has a 50% chance of passing the deleted chromosome to offspring.

typesClassification.type: Alpha thalassemia trait or minor (αα/−− or α+/α+, two genes deleted)
typesClassification.description: Two genes deleted, either in cis on the same chromosome (common in Southeast Asia) or in trans on opposite chromosomes (common in Africa). Causes mild microcytic hypochromic anemia (hemoglobin 10–13 g/dL, MCV 70–80 fL) with normal ferritin. Clinically asymptomatic; sometimes mistaken for iron deficiency.

typesClassification.type: Hemoglobin H disease (α+/−−, three genes deleted)
typesClassification.description: Three of four alpha-globin genes are non-functional. Moderate hemolytic anemia (hemoglobin 7–10 g/dL) with splenomegaly, jaundice, and HbH inclusions in red cells visible on brilliant cresyl blue staining. Exacerbations during infections, oxidant drugs, and pregnancy may require transfusion.

typesClassification.type: Hemoglobin Bart hydrops fetalis (−−/−−, all four genes deleted)
typesClassification.description: Complete absence of alpha-globin chain production. HbBart (γ₄) binds oxygen with very high affinity, delivering none to tissues. Severe intrauterine hypoxia causes massive hydrops (ascites, pleural effusions, edema) and usually stillbirth or neonatal death within hours. Maternal pre-eclampsia occurs in up to 50% of affected pregnancies.

typesClassification.type: Hemoglobin Constant Spring and non-deletion alpha thalassemia
typesClassification.description: Point mutations (most commonly Hb Constant Spring, a stop-codon mutation in HBA2) produce elongated, unstable alpha-globin chains. Combined with a deletion alpha thalassemia allele, Hb CS causes a moderately severe HbH disease variant with greater tendency toward chronic hemolysis.

primarySymptoms: Chronic fatigue and reduced exercise tolerance from persistent mild-to-moderate anemia; most pronounced in HbH disease where hemoglobin ranges from 7 to 10 g/dL.
Pallor of the skin, conjunctivae, and nail beds reflecting low hemoglobin, often present from infancy in HbH disease.
Jaundice (yellowish skin and sclera) from chronic hemolysis and elevated indirect bilirubin in HbH disease; may deepen during infections.
Splenomegaly with left-sided abdominal fullness or discomfort due to extramedullary erythropoiesis and red cell trapping in the spleen.
Gallstones from chronic hemolysis and elevated bilirubin production; biliary colic may first bring HbH patients to medical attention in early adulthood.
Frontal bossing and mild facial bone prominence from medullary expansion in more severe HbH disease, though less severe than in beta thalassemia major.
Growth retardation and delayed puberty in children with poorly controlled HbH disease who require regular transfusions.
Leg ulcers over bony prominences in severely anemic individuals from poor tissue oxygenation and impaired wound healing.
Worsening anemia and jaundice during acute infections or febrile illnesses — hemolytic crises triggered by oxidant stress from concurrent illness.
Fetal hydrops in utero: generalized edema, ascites, pleural effusions, and cardiac failure leading to stillbirth or death in Hb Bart hydrops fetalis.
earlyWarningSigns: Microcytic hypochromic anemia on CBC (MCV below 80 fL, MCH below 27 pg) with normal or elevated serum ferritin — the pattern that distinguishes thalassemia trait from iron deficiency.
Family history of microcytic anemia or thalassemia, especially in individuals of Southeast Asian, Chinese, Mediterranean, Middle Eastern, or African descent.
Mild but persistent anemia in an infant that does not respond to iron supplementation.
Splenomegaly detected on routine examination in a young person of relevant ethnic background with anemia.
Hemoglobin electrophoresis showing reduced or absent HbA₂ (unusual finding that prompts alpha-globin gene testing).
emergencySigns: Fever with sudden worsening of pallor and jaundice — acute hemolytic crisis requiring urgent CBC, reticulocyte count, and possible emergency transfusion.
Acute severe anemia with hemoglobin below 5 g/dL and cardiovascular compromise (tachycardia, hypotension) in HbH disease during infection.

Fetal hydrops detected on antenatal ultrasound (increased nuchal translucency, ascites, pleural effusions) at 12–18 weeks — maternal emergency for risk of pre-eclampsia and need for intrauterine transfusion decision.
Aplastic crisis following parvovirus B19 infection: sudden reticulocytopenia superimposed on chronic hemolysis may cause life-threatening hemoglobin drop.
Signs of biliary obstruction or cholangitis (right-upper-quadrant pain, fever, jaundice) from cholelithiasis in adults with HbH disease.

causes.cause: Large deletions removing one or both alpha-globin genes (deletion alpha thalassemia)
causes.description: The most common cause globally. The −α3.7 deletion (rightward deletion, removing one gene) accounts for most African and Mediterranean cases. The −−SEA deletion (Southeast Asian deletion, removing both genes) is the predominant cause of HbH disease and Hb Bart hydrops fetalis in East and Southeast Asia. Deletions arise by unequal crossover between highly homologous alpha-globin gene regions.

causes.cause: Point mutations in alpha-globin genes (non-deletion alpha thalassemia)
causes.description: Hemoglobin Constant Spring (αCS) involves a stop-codon mutation at position 142 of HBA2, producing a longer unstable chain. Non-deletion alleles are common in Southeast Asia and the Middle East. When inherited with a deletion allele on the opposite chromosome, they cause a moderately severe HbH disease variant with more chronic hemolysis than deletion-only HbH.

causes.cause: Autosomal recessive inheritance pattern requiring both parents to be carriers
causes.description: Alpha thalassemia follows autosomal recessive inheritance. Both parents must carry at least one alpha thalassemia allele for offspring to develop significant disease. Two parents both heterozygous for the −−SEA deletion have a 25% risk per pregnancy of having a Hb Bart hydrops fetalis infant. Genetic counseling and prenatal testing are essential in high-risk couples.

causes.cause: High-prevalence geographic distribution driven by malaria selection pressure
causes.description: Alpha thalassemia trait confers approximately 50% protection against severe Plasmodium falciparum malaria — a selection pressure that drove gene frequencies to 20–30% in malaria-endemic regions of sub-Saharan Africa and Southeast Asia (Williams et al. PLoS Med 2005, PMID 15941360). This evolutionary history explains the current global distribution of carriers.

causes.cause: Acquired alpha thalassemia (ATR-X syndrome and MDS-associated alpha thalassemia)
causes.description: Rarely, alpha thalassemia results from somatic or germline ATRX gene mutations (X-linked intellectual disability-alpha thalassemia syndrome) rather than alpha-globin deletions. In older men, somatic ATRX mutations in myeloid cells cause acquired alpha thalassemia-myelodysplastic syndrome (ATMDS), with HbH inclusions on blood film and concurrent MDS features.

riskFactors.factor: Southeast Asian, Chinese, or Pacific Islander ancestry
riskFactors.description: The −−SEA deletion frequency in Thailand, southern China, and the Philippines reaches 5–10% in the general population. Couples both of Southeast Asian descent have a substantial risk of HbH disease or Hb Bart hydrops fetalis offspring.

The −α3.7 single-gene deletion frequency in West and East Africa reaches 25–30%. African-ancestry individuals rarely develop HbH disease (because the African deletion is almost always in trans), but are common silent carriers who may partner with higher-risk genotypes in multiethnic societies.

Cyprus, Sardinia, and Greece have carrier rates of 10–15% for both alpha and beta thalassemia. Middle Eastern populations carry alpha thalassemia at 3–8% frequency and are at risk for compound heterozygous HbH disease.

riskFactors.factor: Family history of thalassemia or microcytic anemia
riskFactors.description: First-degree relatives of confirmed alpha thalassemia patients or trait carriers have a 50% probability of carrying the same allele. Cascade testing of siblings and parents of known carriers is standard practice in high-risk populations.

riskFactors.factor: Both parents being alpha thalassemia carriers (compound risk)
riskFactors.description: If both parents carry the cis double-deletion (−−SEA), each pregnancy has a 25% risk of Hb Bart hydrops fetalis. If both carry single deletions in trans (common in Africa), HbH disease is very unlikely but the alpha thalassemia trait risk per child is 25%.

riskFactors.factor: Iron deficiency during pregnancy masking the diagnosis
riskFactors.description: Concurrent iron deficiency normalizes red cell indices, obscuring the microcytic pattern and delaying detection of the alpha thalassemia carrier state in pregnant women — a missed opportunity for pre-natal counseling.

riskFactors.factor: Older male age with myelodysplastic syndrome
riskFactors.description: Acquired alpha thalassemia in MDS predominantly affects men over 65 with ATRX somatic mutations; the presence of HbH inclusions in a man with unexplained refractory anemia warrants MDS evaluation.

affectedDemographics: Southeast Asia accounts for the largest absolute burden; Thailand, Laos, Cambodia, southern China, and the Philippines have carrier frequencies of 20–30% in general populations.
Sub-Saharan Africa: single-gene deletion (−α3.7) frequency of 25–40% in many West and East African populations; rarely causes HbH disease but produces millions of silent carriers.
Mediterranean: combined alpha- and beta-thalassemia carrier rates of 10–20% in Cyprus, Sardinia, and Greece drive national screening programs.
United States: approximately 1.5 million alpha thalassemia trait carriers, disproportionately concentrated in communities of Southeast Asian, African American, and Latino descent (CDC data).
Hemoglobin Bart hydrops fetalis: almost exclusively in Southeast Asian populations where the −−SEA deletion predominates; rare outside Asia except in diaspora communities.
diagnosisOverview:
Diagnosis begins with a complete blood count in any person with unexplained microcytic or normocytic anemia. Alpha thalassemia produces low MCV (often 60–75 fL), low MCH (below 27 pg), elevated RBC count, and normal to minimally reduced hemoglobin in the trait, with more pronounced anemia in HbH disease. The critical distinction from iron deficiency is a normal or elevated serum ferritin and normal serum iron studies. Hemoglobin analysis by HPLC or capillary electrophoresis is the next step; in alpha thalassemia, HbA₂ is normal or reduced (unlike beta thalassemia minor where HbA₂ exceeds 3.5%), and HbH (β₄) may be detected as a fast-moving band in HbH disease. Brilliant cresyl blue (BCB) staining of peripheral blood reveals HbH inclusion bodies in red cells — a characteristic but not highly sensitive finding in HbH disease.

Definitive diagnosis requires alpha-globin gene analysis: gap-PCR identifies common large deletions (−α3.7, −α4.2, −−SEA, −−FIL, −−THAI, −−MED), while sequencing detects non-deletion alleles such as Hb Constant Spring. Molecular diagnosis is essential for genetic counseling, prenatal testing, and characterization of severe disease risk in couples. Prenatal diagnosis uses chorionic villus sampling at 11–13 weeks or amniocentesis at 15–18 weeks to identify Hb Bart hydrops fetalis in high-risk pregnancies. Fetal ultrasound findings of hydrops or increased placental thickness at the end of the first trimester trigger urgent molecular confirmation.

diagnosticTests.test: Complete blood count (CBC) with red cell indices
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies microcytic hypochromic anemia and the high RBC count–to–hemoglobin ratio characteristic of alpha thalassemia
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A routine venous blood draw with results in 1–2 hours. An MCV below 80 fL with normal or elevated RBC count in a person of Southeast Asian, African, or Mediterranean descent should prompt further workup.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Distinguishes alpha thalassemia (normal ferritin) from iron-deficiency anemia (low ferritin) — the most common differential diagnosis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Venous blood draw; results in 24 hours. Concurrent iron deficiency can co-exist with thalassemia, particularly in pregnant women; both conditions must then be treated.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects HbH band, quantifies HbA₂ (low or normal in alpha thalassemia), and identifies HbBart in neonates
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw; results in 2–5 days. In the newborn screening context, elevated HbBart percentage at birth correlates with the number of deleted genes — above 25% HbBart indicates Hb Bart hydrops fetalis risk.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Definitively characterizes which alpha-globin genes are deleted or mutated and identifies the cis vs trans configuration of deletions
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood or buccal swab sent to a molecular genetics reference laboratory. Results in 2–4 weeks. Required for prenatal counseling and carrier cascade testing; identifies specific deletions and non-deletion alleles including Hb Constant Spring.

diagnosticTests.test: Brilliant cresyl blue (BCB) peripheral blood smear
diagnosticTests.purpose: Visualizes HbH inclusion bodies (blue-staining insoluble precipitates of β₄ tetramers) in red cells of HbH disease patients
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood smear prepared with brilliant cresyl blue stain; result same day. Sensitivity is moderate — positive in most HbH disease patients but not reliable in mild cases. Complements but does not replace molecular testing.

diagnosticTests.test: Prenatal testing (CVS or amniocentesis with alpha-globin gene analysis)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Diagnoses Hb Bart hydrops fetalis (all 4 genes deleted) in utero in high-risk pregnancies for informed pregnancy management decision
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: CVS at 11–13 weeks or amniocentesis at 15–18 weeks under ultrasound guidance. Fetal DNA is extracted from chorionic villi or amniocytes and analyzed by gap-PCR. Results in 5–10 working days. Both procedures carry a small procedural miscarriage risk (0.5–1%).

treatmentOverview:
Treatment strategy is tailored to genotype and clinical severity. Silent carriers and alpha thalassemia trait require no specific treatment; genetic counseling and pre-conception carrier screening are the key interventions. Patients and partners should be informed of the inheritance risk, particularly regarding the possibility of HbH disease or Hb Bart hydrops fetalis in offspring.

Hemoglobin H disease is managed with a watch-and-support approach during stable periods. Folic acid supplementation (5 mg daily) is recommended for all patients with ongoing hemolysis to maintain erythropoiesis. Avoidance of oxidant drugs (dapsone, sulfonamides, primaquine, certain antimalarials, nitrofurantoin) prevents acute hemolytic exacerbations, as HbH is unstable when oxidized. Prompt antibiotic treatment of infections limits hemolytic crises. Episodic red cell transfusion is given during acute hemolytic exacerbations and in HbH disease patients with hemoglobin below 7 g/dL that causes symptoms or cardiac compromise. The transfusion threshold is individualized, balancing transfusion burden against patient's clinical status.

A minority of HbH disease patients develop transfusion dependency; for these patients — particularly those with Hb Constant Spring genotype or non-deletion forms — long-term transfusion programs with chelation therapy (deferasirox, deferiprone, or deferoxamine) mirror the management of beta thalassemia major. Splenectomy is considered when splenomegaly causes mechanical pain, hypersplenism worsens anemia, or transfusion requirements become excessive; vaccination against encapsulated bacteria before splenectomy is mandatory. Allogeneic HSCT offers cure for HbH-CS patients with severe transfusion-dependent disease who have a matched sibling donor. Hb Bart hydrops fetalis, historically uniformly lethal, has been successfully treated with intrauterine transfusions in specialized fetal medicine centers, with postnatal HSCT as a curative option in those who survive delivery.

medicalTreatments.name: Folic acid supplementation (5 mg daily oral)
medicalTreatments.description: Provides the increased folate required for accelerated erythropoiesis in chronic hemolytic anemia. Recommended lifelong in all HbH disease patients; given throughout pregnancy in all alpha thalassemia carriers.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Prevents megaloblastic crisis superimposed on hemolytic anemia; no randomized trials quantify the increment, but clinical guidelines universally recommend it

medicalTreatments.name: Red cell transfusion (episodic or regular)
medicalTreatments.description: Episodic transfusion in HbH disease corrects severe anemia during acute crises (infections, surgery, pregnancy). Regular transfusion is required in minority of HbH patients with chronic severe anemia (hemoglobin < 7 g/dL with symptoms).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Restores hemoglobin to target 10–12 g/dL within 24 hours; frequency of episodic transfusion in HbH disease averages 1–3 times per year in moderate disease

medicalTreatments.name: Iron chelation therapy (deferasirox 14–28 mg/kg/day oral, or deferoxamine 20–40 mg/kg subcutaneous infusion)
medicalTreatments.description: Prevents transfusional iron overload in patients receiving regular transfusions. Deferasirox is preferred for its once-daily oral dosing; deferoxamine is used in young children or where deferasirox is contraindicated.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces serum ferritin to target below 1,000 µg/L and liver iron below 3 mg/g dry weight with adherent therapy; chelation prevents cardiac iron overload and cardiomyopathy

medicalTreatments.name: Vaccination against encapsulated bacteria prior to splenectomy (or prophylactic penicillin post-splenectomy)
medicalTreatments.description: Pneumococcal (Pneumovax 23 and PCV13), meningococcal ACWY and B, and Haemophilus influenzae type b vaccines reduce risk of overwhelming post-splenectomy sepsis. Penicillin V 250–500 mg twice daily is continued for at least 2 years post-splenectomy and often lifelong.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Overwhelming post-splenectomy sepsis mortality reduced from roughly 50% to under 1% with vaccination and prophylactic antibiotics

medicalTreatments.name: Hydroxyurea (10–20 mg/kg/day oral)
medicalTreatments.description: An off-label option investigated in HbH-Constant Spring patients who have characteristics similar to beta thalassemia intermedia. Increases HbF production, partially compensating for alpha-chain deficit. Limited evidence base from small studies.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Small case series report hemoglobin increments of 1–2 g/dL and reduced transfusion requirements in HbH-CS; formal randomized evidence is lacking

surgicalOptions.name: Splenectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Indicated for HbH disease patients with mechanical splenomegaly causing pain or early satiety, hypersplenism worsening anemia, or escalating transfusion requirements. Laparoscopic approach preferred. Preceded by vaccinations and followed by lifelong antibiotic prophylaxis.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Hemoglobin typically rises 1–2 g/dL post-splenectomy; transfusion requirements reduced in 60–80% of selected patients; thrombosis risk increases post-splenectomy and requires vigilance

surgicalOptions.name: Allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplantation
surgicalOptions.description: Curative option for transfusion-dependent HbH disease patients, particularly HbH-Constant Spring, with a matched sibling donor. Most commonly offered to children to minimize conditioning-related morbidity.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Overall survival above 90% and thalassemia-free survival of 75–85% in well-selected pediatric HbH patients transplanted from an MSD at experienced centers (limited data from Thailand and China)

alternativeTreatments.name: Preimplantation genetic testing (PGT-M)
alternativeTreatments.description: Couples at risk of Hb Bart hydrops fetalis or severe HbH disease can undergo in-vitro fertilization with preimplantation genetic testing to select unaffected embryos before transfer. Offered in specialist reproductive genetics centers; avoids the need for prenatal diagnosis and termination of affected pregnancies.

whySeeSpecialist: A hematologist should evaluate any patient with unexplained microcytic anemia and normal ferritin, particularly in those of high-risk ancestry. Molecular confirmation of the alpha-globin genotype by a genetics laboratory is required for accurate counseling. Maternal-fetal medicine or clinical genetics specialist is essential for couples where both partners are carriers of cis double-deletion alleles.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a hematologist with experience in hemoglobin disorders, ideally at a center affiliated with a thalassemia program or hemoglobinopathy network. For prenatal diagnosis, referral to a maternal-fetal medicine unit with expertise in fetal hydrops and intrauterine transfusion is required when both parents carry high-risk genotypes.
hospitalCriteria: Hemoglobin analysis by HPLC or capillary electrophoresis available on-site
Access to molecular genetics laboratory for alpha-globin gene deletion and mutation analysis
Thalassemia or hemoglobinopathy specialist clinic with nurse coordinator
Transfusion service with leukodepleted, extended antigen-matched red cell products
Maternal-fetal medicine unit for intrauterine transfusion in Hb Bart hydrops fetalis
keyFacilities: Academic hematology centers with thalassemia programs
Fetal medicine units at tertiary obstetric hospitals
Allogeneic HSCT centers for transfusion-dependent HbH disease
preventionStrategies: Universal newborn screening using hemoglobin analysis by HPLC identifies HbBart at birth and enables early genetic counseling for families.
Pre-pregnancy carrier screening for couples of high-risk ancestry (Southeast Asian, Mediterranean, Middle Eastern, African) allows informed reproductive choices before conception.

Genetic counseling for couples where both partners are confirmed carriers — particularly if one partner carries a cis double deletion — to explain the risk of Hb Bart hydrops fetalis and prenatal testing options.
Prenatal diagnosis by CVS or amniocentesis in at-risk pregnancies enables early identification of Hb Bart hydrops fetalis and guides counseling on pregnancy management.
Preimplantation genetic testing in IVF cycles for couples unwilling to consider selective termination.
lifestyleModifications: Avoid known oxidant drugs (dapsone, primaquine, sulfonamides, nitrofurantoin) that precipitate acute hemolytic exacerbations in HbH disease.
Seek prompt medical review for any fever or infection, which can rapidly worsen anemia in HbH disease.
Take prescribed folic acid supplementation daily to support red cell production.
Attend regular hematology follow-up for CBC, ferritin, and spleen size assessment at least annually in HbH disease.
Carry a medical alert card identifying the hemoglobin genotype, especially during travel to areas where emergency care may not recognize thalassemia.
Discuss iron supplementation with a hematologist before taking it; excess iron is harmful in HbH disease except when genuine co-existing iron deficiency is documented.

recommended: Folate-rich foods (lentils, leafy greens, fortified cereals, avocado) to support erythropoiesis
Calcium-rich foods (dairy, fortified plant milks) and vitamin D supplementation if bone density is reduced from chronic anemia or transfusion-related hyperparathyroidism
Adequate calories and protein for growth in children with HbH disease
Hydration of at least 2 L/day to support renal clearance of hemolysis byproducts
avoid: Iron supplements unless serum ferritin is low and a hematologist has recommended them, to avoid iron overload
High-iron foods in excessive amounts in patients who are not iron deficient
Alcohol in excess, which worsens hemolysis and is hepatotoxic with concurrent iron overload

exerciseGuidelines: Silent carriers and alpha thalassemia trait patients have normal exercise tolerance and no activity restrictions. HbH disease patients with hemoglobin above 8 g/dL can participate in moderate aerobic activity but should avoid vigorous exertion when symptomatic during hemolytic crises. During recovery from acute anemia, a gradual return to activity over 2–4 weeks is recommended.
dailyManagement: Take folic acid 5 mg each morning as part of a daily routine.
Record any new symptoms daily — increased pallor, jaundice, darker urine, or fever — to enable timely self-referral.
Check hemoglobin at scheduled intervals (every 3–6 months for HbH disease); contact the care team if hemoglobin falls more than 2 g/dL from baseline.
Maintain an up-to-date list of current medications and share it at every medical encounter to screen for inadvertent oxidant drug prescriptions.
In transfusion-dependent patients, record the volume and date of each transfusion and report signs of iron overload (fatigue, abdominal pain, right-upper-quadrant fullness).
prognosis: Prognosis depends almost entirely on genotype. Silent carriers and alpha thalassemia trait patients have a normal life expectancy and no significant morbidity from the condition itself. HbH disease caused by two deletion alleles is generally mild; most patients maintain hemoglobin above 7–8 g/dL and do not require regular transfusions, with a life expectancy approaching normal when complications are managed. HbH-Constant Spring disease is more severe: approximately 40–60% require at least episodic transfusion during their lifetime, and a minority become transfusion-dependent. Splenectomy reduces transfusion burden in these patients but increases thrombotic risk. Without HSCT, transfusion-dependent HbH patients face progressive iron overload similar to beta thalassemia, with cardiac and endocrine complications limiting lifespan. With optimally managed iron chelation, modern HbH patients achieve good outcomes into adulthood. Hb Bart hydrops fetalis was historically uniformly fatal; with intrauterine transfusion programs in specialized centers, survival to delivery is possible, with HSCT offering long-term cure in survivors.
recoveryTimeline: Hemolytic crises in HbH disease typically resolve over 1–3 weeks after treatment of the precipitating infection and supportive transfusion. Post-transfusion hemoglobin response is seen within 24 hours. For HSCT-treated patients, engraftment occurs by day 21–30 with full donor chimerism expected at 3–6 months.
complications: Iron overload from chronic hemolysis and/or transfusions — detected by serum ferritin above 1,000 µg/L and liver MRI T2* quantification; requires chelation to prevent cardiac and hepatic damage.
Gallstones (pigment cholelithiasis) from chronic hemolysis — occurs in 20–30% of HbH disease patients; cholecystectomy needed for symptomatic cholelithiasis.
Splenomegaly with hypersplenism exacerbating anemia and causing thrombocytopenia — monitor spleen size by ultrasound; splenectomy when indicated.
Aplastic crisis from parvovirus B19 infection — sudden reticulocytopenia superimposed on chronic hemolysis; may require urgent transfusion.
Thrombosis: post-splenectomy, or related to transfusion-dependent iron overload and free hemoglobin — anticoagulation and aspirin considered in high-risk situations.
Delayed puberty, short stature, and hypogonadism in undertreated transfusion-dependent HbH patients — managed with hormone supplementation and endocrinology referral.

supportResources.name: Thalassemia International Federation (TIF)
supportResources.description: Global nonprofit providing patient education, treatment guidelines, and advocacy for thalassemia patients in over 60 countries.

supportResources.name: CDC Hemoglobinopathies Information
supportResources.description: US Centers for Disease Control and Prevention overview of thalassemia including screening recommendations and state health resources.

supportResources.name: NIH National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute — Thalassemias
supportResources.description: Comprehensive patient-facing information on all thalassemia types, including alpha thalassemia genetics, diagnosis, and treatment options.

supportResources.name: Cooley's Anemia Foundation
supportResources.description: US patient organization supporting thalassemia patients with educational resources, support groups, and research funding for thalassemia treatment advances.

confusedWithConditions.name: Iron-deficiency anemia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both cause microcytic hypochromic anemia, but iron deficiency shows low serum ferritin and low serum iron while alpha thalassemia trait shows normal or elevated ferritin. Hemoglobin electrophoresis and alpha-globin gene testing confirm the distinction. Treating alpha thalassemia trait with iron is ineffective and can cause harm if iron stores are normal.

confusedWithConditions.name: Beta thalassemia minor
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Beta thalassemia minor elevates HbA₂ above 3.5% on hemoglobin analysis — a reliable diagnostic marker. Alpha thalassemia trait shows normal or reduced HbA₂. Both present with microcytic hypochromic anemia, but molecular gene testing targets different chromosomes (chromosome 11 for beta, chromosome 16 for alpha).

confusedWithConditions.name: Hemolytic anemia from other causes
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: HbH disease causes chronic hemolysis similar to autoimmune hemolytic anemia, but the direct antiglobulin test (DAT) is negative in HbH disease, HbH inclusions are present on BCB staining, and hemoglobin HPLC shows the HbH band. Autoimmune hemolytic anemia has a positive DAT and responds to steroids or rituximab.

confusedWithConditions.name: Aplastic anemia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Aplastic anemia causes pancytopenia with a hypocellular marrow and reticulocytopenia. Alpha thalassemia produces isolated or predominant anemia with preserved or elevated reticulocyte count, a hyperplastic erythroid marrow, and characteristic hemoglobin analysis findings. The two conditions require entirely different treatments.

relatedConditions.name: Anemia
relatedConditions.relevance: Alpha thalassemia is a leading inherited cause of microcytic anemia globally; distinguishing it from other anemia subtypes is the first diagnostic step.

relatedConditions.name: Aplastic anemia
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are causes of anemia requiring hematology evaluation; acquired alpha thalassemia in MDS can mimic aplastic anemia presentation.

relatedConditions.name: Acute posthemorrhagic anemia
relatedConditions.relevance: Hemolytic crises in HbH disease can cause acute profound anemia requiring urgent transfusion, sharing management principles with posthemorrhagic anemia.

relatedConditions.name: Allergic purpura
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are rare blood disorders that can present with symptoms overlapping in pediatric patients, requiring specialist evaluation.

faqs.question: What is alpha thalassemia?
faqs.answer: Alpha thalassemia is an inherited blood disorder caused by missing or mutated alpha-globin genes. It reduces the production of hemoglobin, the protein that carries oxygen in red blood cells. Severity ranges from no symptoms (silent carrier) to severe anemia requiring transfusions, depending on how many of the four alpha-globin genes are affected.

faqs.question: What is the difference between alpha and beta thalassemia?
faqs.answer: Alpha thalassemia affects alpha-globin genes on chromosome 16 and is usually caused by deletions. Beta thalassemia affects beta-globin genes on chromosome 11 and is usually caused by point mutations. Both reduce hemoglobin production, but beta thalassemia minor raises HbA2 on electrophoresis while alpha thalassemia does not. Severe forms of both require similar management.

faqs.question: Can you have alpha thalassemia with no symptoms?
faqs.answer: Yes. Silent carriers (one gene deleted) and alpha thalassemia trait (two genes deleted) usually have no symptoms or only mild anemia. Most people in these groups are unaware they carry the condition and discover it only through routine blood tests or family screening.

faqs.question: What is hemoglobin H disease?
faqs.answer: Hemoglobin H disease occurs when three of the four alpha-globin genes are deleted or non-functional. Excess beta-globin chains form unstable HbH tetramers that break down in red blood cells, causing chronic moderate hemolytic anemia (hemoglobin 7–10 g/dL), splenomegaly, and jaundice. Most patients need folic acid and occasional transfusions during crises.

faqs.question: What is hemoglobin Bart hydrops fetalis?
faqs.answer: Hemoglobin Bart hydrops fetalis results from deletion of all four alpha-globin genes. The fetus cannot make functional hemoglobin and develops severe oxygen deprivation, causing generalized edema, heart failure, and usually stillbirth. Intrauterine transfusions in specialized centers can allow survival to delivery, followed by bone marrow transplantation.

faqs.question: How is alpha thalassemia diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis starts with a CBC showing microcytic anemia and normal ferritin. Hemoglobin HPLC or electrophoresis may show HbH in affected patients. Definitive diagnosis requires alpha-globin gene analysis (gap-PCR) to identify specific deletions. For prenatal diagnosis, CVS or amniocentesis provides fetal DNA for gene testing.

faqs.question: Is alpha thalassemia curable?
faqs.answer: Alpha thalassemia trait and silent carriers need no treatment and have normal lives. HbH disease is managed but not cured by standard treatment; allogeneic bone marrow transplantation offers a cure for transfusion-dependent HbH patients with a matched sibling donor. Gene therapy trials for thalassemia are ongoing.

faqs.question: Is alpha thalassemia the same as sickle cell disease?
faqs.answer: No. Alpha thalassemia reduces alpha-globin chain production. Sickle cell disease involves a mutation producing abnormal beta-globin chains that polymerize and deform red blood cells. Both are inherited hemoglobin disorders common in malaria-endemic populations, but their mechanisms, symptoms, and treatments are different.

faqs.question: Who is most at risk for alpha thalassemia?
faqs.answer: People of Southeast Asian, southern Chinese, Filipino, African, Mediterranean, and Middle Eastern ancestry have the highest carrier rates. In Thailand and the Philippines, up to 30% of the general population carries a deletion allele. In the US, approximately 1.5 million people of these backgrounds are alpha thalassemia carriers.

faqs.question: Does alpha thalassemia affect pregnancy?
faqs.answer: Alpha thalassemia trait and HbH disease patients can have successful pregnancies but require closer monitoring. Folic acid supplementation is essential. If both parents are carriers, prenatal testing is advised. Hb Bart hydrops fetalis pregnancies carry a 50% risk of maternal pre-eclampsia and require referral to a high-risk obstetric center.

faqs.question: Do I need a bone marrow transplant for alpha thalassemia?
faqs.answer: Most alpha thalassemia patients do not need a bone marrow transplant. Silent carriers and trait patients need no treatment. Transplantation is reserved for transfusion-dependent HbH disease patients who have a matched sibling donor, typically children with severe HbH-Constant Spring genotype.

faqs.question: What drugs should people with HbH disease avoid?
faqs.answer: People with HbH disease should avoid oxidant drugs that destabilize HbH and trigger hemolytic crises. These include dapsone, primaquine, sulfonamides, nitrofurantoin, and some antimalarial agents. Always inform prescribers of the HbH diagnosis before starting any new medication.

faqs.question: Does alpha thalassemia cause iron deficiency?
faqs.answer: No — alpha thalassemia itself does not cause iron deficiency. Ferritin is typically normal or elevated. A common error is to prescribe iron supplements to an undiagnosed alpha thalassemia trait patient when microcytic anemia is found; iron supplementation does not improve anemia in thalassemia and can cause harm.

faqs.question: Can alpha thalassemia be detected at birth?
faqs.answer: Yes. Newborn screening by hemoglobin analysis (HPLC) detects elevated HbBart at birth; the percentage correlates with the number of deleted genes. More than 25% HbBart indicates possible Hb Bart hydrops fetalis. Most states and countries with hemoglobinopathy screening programs include alpha thalassemia detection.

faqs.question: What is the life expectancy with alpha thalassemia?
faqs.answer: Silent carriers and trait patients have a completely normal life expectancy. HbH disease patients with adequate folic acid and monitoring for complications also approach normal life expectancy with modern management. Transfusion-dependent HbH patients face risks from iron overload if chelation is inadequate, but optimal therapy achieves good long-term outcomes.

metaDescription: Alpha thalassemia: an inherited hemoglobin disorder from deleted alpha-globin genes. Symptoms, types, diagnosis, and treatment explained by medical specialists.
